sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 105, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: In Level 1, absorption happened when W center green moved INTO the dark outline from outside. In Level 2, the W center green is already inside the dark outline. Need to move it OUT first, then back IN to trigger absorption.
- Plan: Move LEFT (out) then RIGHT (back in) to trigger absorption.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 21 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Still no absorption. The state is identical before and after LEFT+RIGHT. The green is back at (39-41, 54-59) inside the dark outline at (38-42, 53-60), and the dark outline still has px=22.
Let me reconsider the Level 1 absorption. In the last Level 1 state, the green with W center was at (33-35, 15-17) and the dark outline was at (32-36, 11-15). The green was to the RIGHT of the dark outline. The action was LEFT, which moved the green into the dark outline.
But wait, the dark outline at (32-36, 11-15) has an irregular shape. Let me look at the boundary: [[32, 11], [32, 15], [32, 12], [33, 11], [35, 11], [36, 12], [36, 15], [36, 11]]. This is not a simple rectangle. It has a "notch" or opening on the right side.
And inside the dark outline, there's w px=9 at (33-35, 12-14), which is light gray. This might be the "hollow center" of the dark outline.
The green with W center was at (33-35, 15-17), which is to the right of the dark outline's hollow center. When the green moved LEFT, it moved into the hollow center of the dark outline.
So the absorption in Level 1 happened when the green moved into the hollow center of the dark outline, not just overlapping with the dark outline.
In Level 2, the W center green is at (39-41, 54-59), which is inside the dark outline at (38-42, 53-60). The dark outline's hollow center would be rows 39-41, cols 54-59, which is exactly where the green is. So the green is already in the hollow center.
But the absorption didn't happen. Maybe the absorption in Level 2 requires a different trigger.
Wait, let me look at the Level 1 state more carefully. The dark outline at (26-30, 44-48) had a green with c center inside it. This green was already absorbed (or maybe it was never supposed to be absorbed, since it had a c center, not a W center).
The dark outline at (32-36, 11-15) had a green with W center next to it. When the green moved LEFT into the dark outline, the level was completed.
So in Level 1, there were 2 dark outlines, but only 1 needed to be absorbed (the one with the W center green). The other dark outline already had a green with c center inside it.
In Level 2, there are 4 dark outlines and 4 greens. Maybe the goal is to absorb all 4 dark outlines by moving the W center green into each one.
But the W center green can only be in one place at a time. So maybe the process is:
1. Move the W center green to a dark outline
2. Absorb the dark outline (but how?)
3. Swap the W center to another green
4. Move that green to its dark outline
5. Repeat
But I've been unable to trigger absorption. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the absorption in Level 2 requires the green to be moved OUT of the dark outline, not into it. Or maybe the absorption requires the green to be moved in a specific direction.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the Level 1 absorption. The green with W center was at (33-35, 15-17), and the dark outline was at (32-36, 11-15). The green was to the RIGHT of the dark outline. The action was LEFT, which moved the green into the dark outline.
But the dark outline had an irregular shape with an opening on the right side. The green moved through the opening into the hollow center.
In Level 2, the dark outline at (38-42, 53-60) is a simple rectangle. The green is already inside the hollow center. Maybe the absorption requires the green to enter through a specific opening, but there's no opening in this dark outline.
Wait, but the dark outline at (38-42, 53-60) is a simple rectangle with boundary [[38, 53], [38, 60], [42, 60], [42, 53]]. This is a 5x8 rectangle. The hollow center would be rows 39-41, cols 54-59, which is a 3x6 rectangle. The green is exactly this size and position.
But the dark outline is a closed rectangle with no openings. How can the green enter or exit?
Actually, looking at the boundary, the dark outline is a 5x8 rectangle. The outer boundary is rows 38-42, cols 53-60. The hollow center is rows 39-41, cols 54-59. The dark outline is the border of this rectangle.
The green is at (39-41, 54-59), which is exactly the hollow center. The green is "inside" the dark outline, but the dark outline is a closed border.
In Level 1, the dark outline had an opening, and the green moved through the opening into the hollow center. In Level 2, the dark outline is closed, and the green is already inside.
Maybe the absorption in Level 2 requires the green to be moved OUT of the dark outline, breaking through the border. Or maybe the absorption requires the green to be in a different position relative to the dark outline.
Let me try moving the green UP, out of the dark outline, and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT+RIGHT didn't trigger absorption. The green is still inside the dark outline with px=22. In Level 1, the dark outline had an opening and the green moved through it. In Level 2, the dark outline is closed and the green is already inside.
Plan: Try moving the green UP out of the dark outline to see if that triggers absorption or changes the dark outline.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# W center green at (39-41, 54-59), dark outline at (38-42, 53-60)
# Move UP to try to exit the dark outline
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'B', 'c') and n['pixels'] <= 32:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
